expressions.dialogue: Jill says vestibule... I'm going with vestibule.
expressions.speaker: Chandler
expressions.meaning: 두 가지 선택지 중 하나를 고르기로 결정하다 (특히 말이나 표현을 선택할 때)
expressions.nuance: 구어체에서 짧고 단호하게 선택을 선언할 때 씀. 1990년대나 지금이나 자연스럽게 쓰이는 표현.
expressions.example: I wasn't sure whether to say 'dinner' or 'supper,' but I'm going with 'dinner.' → '저녁 식사'를 dinner라고 할지 supper라고 할지 몰랐는데, dinner로 정했어.

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: Great story!  But, I uh, I gotta go, I got a date with Andrea--Angela--Andrea...  Oh man, (looks to Chandler)

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: Yes, 'cause now I have to go down there, and deal with them.

occ.speaker: Rachel
occ.text: Oh, yeah, yeah. Actually, I got the extended disco version, with three choruses of "You'll never make it on your own".

expressions.dialogue: you waited too long to make your move, and now you're in the friend zone.
expressions.speaker: Joey
expressions.meaning: (연애나 경쟁에서) 먼저 행동을 취하다, 결정적인 한 수를 두다
expressions.nuance: 연애뿐 아니라 비즈니스 협상이나 게임 전략에서도 씀. 지금도 매우 자주 쓰이는 표현.
expressions.example: She's clearly interested — stop overthinking and just make your move. → 그녀가 분명히 관심 있어 — 너무 많이 생각하지 말고 그냥 먼저 행동해.

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: Yeah! (Monica falls asleep) No! No! No! Don't fall asleep! Okay, I am going to make you some coffee. (Monica doesn't move as he gets out of bed and as he's heading for the door.) And I probably won't spill coffee grounds all over the kitchen floor.

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: Oh, it's so hard to get rid of stuff! Did you and Chandler have to make compromises when you first moved in together?

expressions.dialogue: you waited too long to make your move, and now you're in the friend zone.
expressions.speaker: Joey
expressions.meaning: 상대방이 나를 연인이 아닌 그냥 친구로만 보는 상황에 갇혀 있다
expressions.nuance: 이 에피소드에서 처음 사용된 표현으로, 이후 전 세계적으로 통용되는 말이 됨. 현재도 인터넷 문화에서 밈으로 자주 인용됨.
expressions.example: I've been helping her move apartments twice now — I think I'm deep in the friend zone. → 이사를 두 번이나 도와줬는데, 내가 완전히 프렌드존에 있는 것 같아.

expressions.dialogue: Ross, you're mayor of the zone.
expressions.speaker: Joey
expressions.meaning: 프렌드존에 '있다'는 것도 부족해 — 거기서 가장 깊이 박혀 있다는 뜻의 극도로 강조된 표현
expressions.nuance: 'mayor of X' 패턴은 'X의 대표자/최고 고수'라는 뜻으로 유머러스하게 씀. Joey 특유의 과장법이며 이 패턴 자체는 지금도 응용 가능.
expressions.example: You've canceled on her three times in a row — you're not just in the friend zone, you're the mayor. → 세 번 연속으로 약속을 취소했잖아 — 프렌드존에 있는 게 아니라 거기 시장이지.

expressions.dialogue: I'm taking my time, alright? I'm laying the groundwork.
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.meaning: 나중의 성공을 위해 미리 준비를 천천히 쌓아나가다, 기반을 다지다
expressions.nuance: 비즈니스, 외교, 연애 등 다양한 맥락에서 격식/비격식 모두 자연스러운 표현. 지금도 활발히 사용됨.
expressions.example: We need to spend this quarter laying the groundwork before we pitch to investors. → 투자자에게 제안하기 전에 이번 분기는 기반을 다지는 데 써야 해.

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: Just casually slip it in, y'know lay the groundwork. Tell her uh, I'm a loner-No! An outlaw! Tell her she doesn't want to get mixed up with the likes of me.

expressions.dialogue: OK, here goes. For a while now, I've been wanting to, um....
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.meaning: 긴장하거나 설레는 마음으로 어떤 일을 막 시작하려 할 때 쓰는 말 ('자, 간다' '해보자')
expressions.nuance: 'Here goes nothing'은 잘 안 될 것 같지만 어쨌든 해본다는 뉘앙스를 더함. 단독 'Here goes'는 긴장된 결심의 순간에 씀. 지금도 흔히 씀.
expressions.example: I've never sung in front of a crowd before, but here goes. → 사람들 앞에서 노래해본 적이 없지만, 한번 해보자.

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: I think we are getting a little ahead of selves here. Okay. Okay. I am just going to get up, go to work and not think about him all day. Or else I'm just gonna get up and go to work.

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: What's going on here? You go out with tons of girls.

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: Vell, Eva, ve've done some excellent vork here, and I vould have to say, your pwoblem is qviiite clear. (He goes into a song and dance number.)

expressions.dialogue: It's never gonna happen.
expressions.speaker: Joey
expressions.meaning: 그 일은 절대 일어나지 않을 것이다 — 냉정하고 단호한 부정
expressions.nuance: 'going to'를 'gonna'로 줄이는 것은 구어체에서 매우 일반적. 친구에게 현실을 직설적으로 말할 때 씀. 지금도 매우 자주 쓰임.
expressions.example: He keeps saying he'll call you back, but honestly, it's never gonna happen. → 다시 전화하겠다고 계속 말하는데, 솔직히 그런 일은 절대 없을 거야.

occ.speaker: Ross
occ.text: I know, I know I was, but there was always this little voice inside that kept saying it's never gonna happen, move on. You know whose voice that was?

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: Joey, what are you doing?! It's never gonna happen, she's seeing somebody.

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: It's never gonna happen.

expressions.dialogue: What's messing you up? The wine? The candles? The moonlight?
expressions.speaker: Joey
expressions.meaning: 뭐가 너를 방해하고 있어? 뭐 때문에 못 하고 있어?
expressions.nuance: 'mess up'은 보통 '망치다'이지만 여기서는 '(정신을) 혼란스럽게 하다'는 뜻. 비격식적이며 지금도 자연스럽게 씀.
expressions.example: You've been staring at that email for an hour — what's messing you up? → 한 시간째 그 이메일만 쳐다보고 있잖아 — 뭐가 걸리는 거야?

expressions.dialogue: Mental note: If Jill Goodacre offers you gum, you take it. If she offers you mangled animal carcass, you take it.
expressions.speaker: Chandler
expressions.meaning: 잊지 않도록 머릿속으로 스스로에게 다짐하거나 메모하다
expressions.nuance: 보통 'Mental note:' 뒤에 명령형 문장을 붙임. 유머러스한 맥락에서도 잘 어울리며 지금도 매우 자주 씀.
expressions.example: Mental note: never schedule a 9 AM meeting the day after a holiday. → 정신적 메모: 연휴 다음 날에 오전 9시 미팅 잡지 말 것.

expressions.dialogue: You know, on second thought, gum would be perfection.
expressions.speaker: Chandler
expressions.meaning: 다시 생각해보니, 마음이 바뀌어서 (처음 입장이나 결정을 번복할 때)
expressions.nuance: 격식/비격식 모두 자연스러움. 영국 영어는 'on second thoughts' (복수형). 'On reflection'보다 훨씬 구어적이며 지금도 자주 씀.
expressions.example: I'll take the soup — actually, on second thought, I'll have the salad. → 수프로 할게요 — 아니, 다시 생각해보니 샐러드 주세요.

expressions.dialogue: 'Gum would be perfection'? 'Gum would be perfection.' Could have said 'gum would be nice,' or 'I'll have a stick,' but no, no, no, no. For me, gum is perfection. I loathe myself.
expressions.speaker: Chandler
expressions.meaning: ~이라면 완벽하겠죠 — 평범한 상황에서 지나치게 격식적이거나 과장된 표현
expressions.nuance: 'That would be great' 정도가 자연스러운 상황에 'That would be perfection'이라고 하면 어색하게 과장된 느낌을 줌. 이 에피소드는 'perfection'의 과도한 사용이 얼마나 어색한지를 잘 보여줌.
expressions.example: A: Would you like some water? B: Water would be perfection. (→ 어색함; 'that'd be great' 또는 'yes please'가 자연스러움) → '물 드릴까요?' '물이라면 완벽하겠죠.' (지나치게 격식적인 대답)

expressions.dialogue: For me, gum is perfection. I loathe myself.
expressions.speaker: Chandler
expressions.meaning: 나 자신이 정말 싫다, 내가 너무 한심하다 — 강한 자기혐오
expressions.nuance: 'hate'보다 훨씬 강하고 격식적인 단어 'loathe'를 일상적 자기 비하에 써서 유머 효과를 냄. Chandler 특유의 자기 비하 유머 패턴이며 지금도 이런 용법으로 씀.
expressions.example: I just told my boss his idea was 'interesting.' I loathe myself. → 방금 상사한테 아이디어가 '흥미롭다'고 했어. 나 자신이 너무 싫다.

expressions.dialogue: Rachel and I... we're kind of a thing.
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.meaning: 우리는 어떤 관계 같은 게 있다 — 연인인지 명확하지 않은 관계를 모호하게 표현
expressions.nuance: 'a thing'은 구어체에서 정의하기 어려운 낭만적 관계를 뜻함. 'kind of'를 붙이면 더 불확실함을 더함. 현재도 매우 자주 쓰이는 표현.
expressions.example: Are they dating? — I honestly don't know, I think they're kind of a thing. → 걔네 사귀어? — 솔직히 잘 모르겠어, 뭔가 있는 것 같긴 한데.

occ.speaker: Barry
occ.text: Yeah, well, uh, we're kind of a thing now.

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: Right now, right here. Don't ya think we're in kind of a public plaaaa [Susie grabs him under the table] They do have the shrimp.

expressions.dialogue: Passion is way overrated. It is. Eventually, it kind of... burns out.
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.meaning: (열정이나 감정이) 점점 사그라들다, 소진되다
expressions.nuance: 사람의 '번아웃(burn out)'과 감정/열정이 다 소진될 때 모두 씀. 관계의 열정이 식는다는 맥락에서 쓰일 때는 반드시 부정적이지만은 않음.
expressions.example: The initial excitement of any new project burns out after the first few weeks. → 새 프로젝트에 대한 초반 흥분은 처음 몇 주가 지나면 항상 사그라들어.

occ.speaker: Ross
occ.text: (to Rachel) But work comes first! (to Mark) Oh hey, but that's sad about you though, what happened? Burn out? Burn all out, did ya?

expressions.dialogue: if you get in the... way, of us becoming a thing, then I would be, well, very sad.
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.meaning: (~의) 방해가 되다, (~를) 가로막다
expressions.nuance: 'get in the way' 뒤에 'of + 명사/동명사'를 붙여 무엇의 방해인지 표현함. 물리적 방해와 비유적 방해 모두에 쓰임. 격식/비격식 모두 자연스러움.
expressions.example: Don't let ego get in the way of a good apology. → 자존심이 진심 어린 사과를 방해하게 두지 마.

occ.speaker: Chan
occ.text: Oh, you know, I would, but that might get in the way of my lying around time.

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: Yeah. Except for, y'know when you're on a date and you're getting along really great but the guy's translator keeps getting in the way.

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: Oh, I'm sorry, am I getting in the way of all the room switching fun?

expressions.dialogue: Alright, alright, alright. It's been fourteen and a half minutes and you still have not said one word. Oh God, do something. Just make contact, smile!
expressions.speaker: Chandler (to himself)
expressions.meaning: (어색하거나 긴장된 상황에서) 상대에게 눈을 맞추거나 말을 걸어 처음으로 소통을 시작하다
expressions.nuance: 군사·스포츠 용어에서도 쓰이지만 일상에서는 사회적으로 긴장된 상황에서 '그냥 말이라도 걸어봐'라는 뜻으로 씀.
expressions.example: I was so nervous at the networking event that I couldn't even make contact with anyone. → 네트워킹 행사에서 너무 긴장해서 아무에게도 말을 못 걸었어.

occ.speaker: Ross
occ.text: Okay. (He leans in to kiss her again, but she leans back preventing him from making contact.) Wow, it's-it's 5:30 in the morning. (Rachel laughs) So, I'd better get cracking on this baby.

expressions.dialogue: (smiling) You betcha!
expressions.speaker: Phoebe
expressions.meaning: 물론이지!, 당연하지! — 강하고 따뜻한 긍정
expressions.nuance: 'You bet'의 강조형. 미국 중서부·남부에서 많이 쓰이며 격식 없이 활기차고 친근한 느낌을 줌. 지금도 쓰이지만 약간 구식 또는 과장된 느낌이 있음.
expressions.example: Will you come to my graduation? — You betcha, I wouldn't miss it! → 졸업식에 와줄 거지? — 당연하지, 절대 안 빠질게!

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: You betcha!

occ.speaker: Rachel
occ.text: Yeah, great, you betcha!

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: Yeah I can! (Laughs) And don't think I don't, because I do! I mean all the time, you betcha! (Laughs.)

expressions.expression: end up [doing something / in some situation]
expressions.dialogue: If you don't ask her out soon you're going to end up stuck in the zone forever.
expressions.speaker: Joey
expressions.meaning: 결국 ~하게 되다, ~한 상황에 처하게 되다 (보통 의도치 않은 결과)
expressions.nuance: 계획하지 않거나 원하지 않는 상황에 결국 이르게 될 때 씀. 매우 일상적이고 자주 쓰이는 표현으로 1990년대나 지금이나 동일.
expressions.example: If you keep skipping the gym, you'll end up starting from zero again in January. → 계속 헬스장을 안 가면 결국 1월에 다시 처음부터 시작하게 될 거야.

occ.speaker: Tag
occ.text: A year. On and off. I kinda thought we'd end up together. I don't anymore.

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: Fine, but if we end up not doing this Maxim thing because of this party...

expressions.dialogue: You owe me a cat.
expressions.speaker: Mr. Heckles
expressions.meaning: 너는 나에게 ~을 빚졌다, 나한테 ~을 보상해야 해
expressions.nuance: 물질적 보상뿐 아니라 'You owe me an explanation' (설명해야 해), 'You owe me one' (신세 진 거야) 등으로도 폭넓게 씀. 매우 일상적.
expressions.example: You took the last coffee without making a new pot — you owe me. → 새 커피를 내리지도 않고 마지막 커피를 가져갔잖아 — 나한테 빚진 거야.

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: (to Joey) You owe me 20 bucks.

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: What rule? There's no rule, if anything, you owe me a table!

occ.speaker: Mr. Heckles
occ.text: You owe me a waffle.

expressions.dialogue: Passion is way overrated.
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.meaning: 훨씬, 엄청 — 형용사/부사를 강조하는 구어적 부사
expressions.nuance: 10대·20대 구어체에서 특히 자주 쓰임. 'much more'보다 훨씬 캐주얼함. 'way too much', 'way better', 'way off' 등의 패턴. 1990년대에 유행했고 지금도 여전히 씀.
expressions.example: This place is way cheaper than the one downtown. → 이 곳은 시내에 있는 곳보다 훨씬 저렴해.

occ.speaker: Paul
occ.text: (laughing) That's one way! Me, I- I went for the watch.

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: Monica- Hi! Um, Monica, you're scaring me. I mean, you're like, you're like all chaotic and twirly. And not-not in a good way.

occ.speaker: Ross
occ.text: ...I'll go. Let's start with the way he kept picking at- no, I'm sorry, I can't do this, can't do this. We loved him.

expressions.dialogue: The weirdest place would have to be... (sigh)... oh, the foot of the bed. / Step back.
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.meaning: (놀라움이나 감탄으로) '잠깐만', '대박', '말도 안 돼'
expressions.nuance: 물리적으로 물러서라는 뜻이 아니라 충격이나 감탄의 감탄사로 쓰임. 1990년대~2000년대 구어체에서 많이 쓰였으며 현재는 잘 쓰이지 않음.
expressions.example: You got front-row tickets to that show? Step back! → 그 공연 맨 앞줄 티켓을 구했다고? 말도 안 돼!

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: But this is a two line part, it's like takin' a step backwards. I'm not gonna do this.

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: Ohh! Umm, Phoebe, I kinda need to talk to you about that. (Rachel excuses herself) Umm, well I-I-I think it might be time for me to take a step back from catering.

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: Here's your tea Phoebe. (They give it to her and quickly take a step back.)

expressions.dialogue: I don't know, some guy. / 'Hey Jill, I saw you with some guy last night. Yes, he was some guy.'
expressions.speaker: Jill / Chandler
expressions.meaning: 어떤 남자/여자 — 이름도 중요하지 않다는 듯 무심하게 상대를 가리키는 표현
expressions.nuance: 'some guy'는 상대를 중요하지 않게 취급하거나 잘 모른다는 뉘앙스를 가짐. Chandler가 'some guy'로 불린 것에 내심 상처받는 장면이 이 표현의 무게를 잘 보여줌.
expressions.example: Who was that at the door? — Oh, just some guy selling magazines. → 문에 온 사람이 누구야? — 아, 잡지 파는 어떤 사람이야.

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: There's nothing to tell! He's just some guy I work with!

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: What? So you guys don't mind going out with someone else who's going out with someone else?

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: I dunno. Some fat guy's sleeping with the store manager. He's not even jolly, it's all political.

expressions.expression: That is the part to focus on. (sarcastic)
expressions.dialogue: Oh, yeah, that is the part to focus on, you idiot!
expressions.speaker: Chandler (to himself)
expressions.meaning: 정말 중요한 걸 놔두고 사소한 것에 집착하는 자신을 비꼬는 자기 풍자적 표현
expressions.nuance: 자기 자신 또는 타인을 향한 날카로운 반어법. 'That's really what you're focused on right now?'와 같은 뉘앙스. 지금도 유머러스한 자기 비판에 씀.
expressions.example: My flight got canceled but hey, at least the airport lounge had great WiFi. Yeah, that's the part to focus on. → 비행기가 취소됐지만 공항 라운지 와이파이는 좋았어. 그래, 그게 중요하지.

expressions.dialogue: Good save! We're back on track, and I'm... (grimacing) ...chewing someone else's gum.
expressions.speaker: Chandler (to himself)
expressions.meaning: 잘 수습했어!, 잘 만회했어! — 실수나 어색한 순간을 잘 넘긴 것을 인정할 때
expressions.nuance: 골키퍼가 골을 막아낸다는 스포츠 용어에서 온 표현. 대화에서 실수 후 재치 있게 넘어갔을 때 씀. 지금도 자주 쓰임.
expressions.example: You forgot his name but called him 'man' like you meant it? Good save. → 이름을 잊어버렸는데 '친구'라고 자연스럽게 불렀어? 잘 넘겼네.

expressions.dialogue: You'd better act surprised.
expressions.speaker: Joey
expressions.meaning: 놀란 척하다, 미리 알고 있지만 모르는 척 놀라는 연기를 하다
expressions.nuance: 'act + adjective' 패턴 — 'act innocent', 'act casual', 'act normal' 등. 비밀이 미리 새어나갔을 때 쓰는 매우 일상적인 표현으로 지금도 자주 씀.
expressions.example: They're throwing you a surprise party on Friday — just act surprised when you walk in. → 금요일에 깜짝 파티를 열어줄 거야 — 들어갈 때 그냥 놀란 척해.

occ.speaker: Mrs. Geller
occ.text: Well sweetie, we have a surprise for you. We're turning your room into a gym.

occ.speaker: Eddie
occ.text: I got a little surprise, look. There's a new fishie. I named him uh, Chandler, you know, after, after you.

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: No, no, not a party. Just a surprise gathering of some people Rachel knows. Um, this is Phoebe and Chandler and Joey.
